title: Making leaf mould from autumn leaves
subtitle: Wire cage or bin bags for a year-round supply of fine soil conditioner
excerpt: Leaf mould is decomposed leaf matter, not compost, but a fine-textured soil conditioner and mulch. Autumn leaves collected into a wire cage or bin bags with a few holes take 12-24 months to break down to a usable material. It costs nothing but time and a roll of chicken wire.
sourceNotes: RHS leaf mould guidance (rhs.org.uk). Garden Organic leaf mould factsheet.

content.text: is broken down by fungi, not bacteria. That is why the process is slower and cooler than composting, and why leaves should be kept apart from the main compost heap. The result after 12 months is a part-broken-down material good for mulching. After 24 months it is fine-textured enough to use in a seed-sowing mix.

Knock four stakes into the ground in a 1 m square and wrap chicken wire or wire mesh around them. Fill with autumn leaves, compacting them as you go. The cage holds the pile together, allows air and rain in through the wire, and is large enough to insulate itself slightly. Do not add other compost materials.

Fill black bin bags with damp leaves (wet them if freshly collected on a dry day), tie loosely, and pierce a dozen holes in each bag. Stack in a corner and leave for 12-24 months. The bags retain moisture and accelerate the fungal process relative to a dry, exposed cage.
Which leaves and how to collect
Most deciduous leaves work: oak, beech, hornbeam, and hazel break down in 12-18 months. Plane and sycamore take slightly longer.
Evergreen leaves (laurel, holly, pine needles) are waxy and slow, avoid or shred first.
Shredding or mowing over leaves before bagging halves the decomposition time.
Leaves swept from pavements and roads are fine to use. There is no clear evidence that street lighting or vehicle pollution affects the final product in a meaningful way.
12-month mould: use as a 50-75 mm surface mulch around shrubs and perennials to retain moisture and suppress weeds.
24-month mould: sieve and use as 20-30% of a seed-sowing compost mix.
